demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.a_chall: A challenger disputes and posts their bond.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.a_propo: A proposer posts a claim about the final state (with a bond).
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.an_asse: An assertion is a claim about the rollup state after executing a batch (e.g., a state root). It is optimistic: it is accepted unless successfully challenged within the dispute window.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.arbitru: Arbitrum uses an interactive dispute game to keep L1 verification costs low: only a single step is checked on-chain at the end.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.at_this: At this point, L1 only needs to check a single transition. If claimed and true results differ, the assertion is fraudulent.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.automat: Automatically run the bisection rounds to reach a 1-step dispute interval (hi = lo + 1), ready for the final on-chain check.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.challen: Challenge the assertion by posting a challenger bond. This starts the interactive dispute game (bisection).
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.commit_: Commit a midpoint state for the currently disputed interval. The challenger then picks the half-range where the mismatch lies (binary search).
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.dispute: Dispute range is 1 step - ready for one-step proof on L1.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.enterin: Entering interactive bisection (binary search) to find the first disputed step.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.ethereu: Ethereum (L1) verifies a single step on-chain to resolve the dispute.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.fraud_p: Fraud proofs let rollups inherit Ethereum security without re-executing everything on L1. Most transactions happen off-chain; L1 is the court of final appeal.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.green_s: Green = states match, Red = mismatch. Highlighted = currently disputed interval.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.highlig: Highlighted rows are inside the current disputed interval [lo..hi].
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.how_man: How many execution steps are in the simulated trace. More steps means more bisection rounds to isolate a single disputed step.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.how_the: How the Arbitrum-style dispute game works
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.injecte: Injected incorrect transition at step {{step}} (first wrong state is index {{idx}}).
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.instead: Instead of re-executing the whole batch on L1, bisection narrows the disagreement to one step. This keeps verification cheap while still catching invalid state transitions.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.mismatc: Mismatch at this step — fraud proof will succeed.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.no_even: No events yet. Start by posting an assertion.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.no_mism: No mismatch at this step — challenger loses if executed now.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.onestep: One-step proof can only run when hi = lo + 1.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.post_an: Post an assertion (claim) about the final state after executing the rollup batch. This locks the proposer bond and starts the dispute window.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.rollup_: Rollup safety depends on at least one honest challenger. In practice, teams run monitoring + challenger infrastructure to react quickly.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.simulat: Simulate an interactive fraud proof: a proposer posts an assertion, a challenger disputes, and both bisect the execution trace until L1 verifies a single step.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.state_i: State index i refers to the state *after* i steps of execution. The transition from index i to i+1 is one execution step (and uses input[i]).
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.the_dis: The dispute range [lo..hi] refers to state indices. When it shrinks to a single transition (hi = lo + 1), L1 can verify that one step to decide the winner.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.the_per: The per-step input used to compute the next state for the transition i → i+1. (The last state has no input.)
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.the_tru: The true state value produced by the honest execution trace (ground truth in this simulation).
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.they_re: They repeatedly bisect the execution trace to isolate the first disputed step.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.this_ba: This bar visualizes the current disputed interval [lo..hi] over the execution trace. Each bisection round shrinks the highlighted segment until only one step remains for the one-step proof.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.this_is: This is the current disputed interval. Bisection keeps shrinking it until only one transition remains (hi = lo + 1) for the one-step proof.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.whether: Whether the claimed and true states are equal at this index. A mismatch indicates fraud somewhere in the disputed interval.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.withdra: Withdrawals are delayed by a challenge period. If an invalid assertion is posted, challengers can dispute before funds finalize.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.bisecti: bisection rounds → one-step proof on L1
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto.fast_l2: fast L2 UX + delayed L1 finality
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto._challe: ⚔️ Challenger disputes the assertion and posts a bond.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto._fraud_: ✅ Fraud proven! Proposer loses bond; challenger wins.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto._no_fra: ❌ No fraud: challenger was wrong and loses their bond.
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto._propos: 📌 Proposer posts an assertion: state[0] → state[{{n}}].
demos.fraud-proofs-arbitrum.auto._l1_ver: 🔎 L1 verifies a single step: state[{{i}}] → state[{{j}}]
